title: Install Pygame & Open a Window
html:
Why Pygame

Pygame is a thin, friendly wrapper over SDL2. It gives you a window, a frame buffer (the display surface), input events, image/sound loading, and timing. It does not give you a scene tree, a physics engine, or an editor. That is the point: in Python with Pygame you write the engine yourself, which is the fastest way to truly understand how games work under the hood.

Install a clean environment first

Always work inside a virtual environment so your game's dependencies never collide with other Python projects. Create one, activate it, then install the maintained pygame-ce (Community Edition) fork, which is the actively developed version most tutorials now target.

terminal
$ python3 -m venv .venv $ source .venv/bin/activate # macOS/Linux $ .venv\Scripts\activate # Windows (PowerShell) $ pip install pygame-ce $ python -m pygame.examples.aliens # sanity check: a game runs
pygame vs pygame-ce.
They share the same import pygame name, so you can only install one at a time. pygame-ce ships more features and fixes; if you already had pygame, run pip uninstall pygame first.

The four steps every Pygame program needs

Every Pygame app follows the same shape: initialize, create a display, run a loop that drains events and draws, then quit. Here is the smallest program that opens a real, titled, closable window.

main.py
import pygame, sys pygame.init() # start all subsystems screen = pygame.display.set_mode((800, 600)) # the display surface pygame.display.set_caption("My First Window") # title bar text running = True while running: for event in pygame.event.get(): # MUST drain the queue if event.type == pygame.QUIT: # the X / Cmd-Q running = False screen.fill((30, 30, 46)) # R,G,B (0-255) pygame.display.flip() # show the new frame pygame.quit() # release the window sys.exit()

What each line does

  • pygame.init() boots every subsystem (video, font, mixer). It returns a tuple of (successes, failures); you can ignore it for now.
  • set_mode((w, h)) returns the display surface — the canvas the window shows. You draw to it, then call flip() to present it.
  • pygame.event.get() empties the OS event queue. If you never read it, the OS thinks your app has frozen and the window becomes unresponsive (the dreaded "not responding").
  • fill + flip is the minimal render: paint the whole surface, then swap it onto the screen.
The single most common beginner bug.
If your window opens then immediately closes, or hangs and shows a beachball/spinner, you almost certainly forgot the for event in pygame.event.get() loop. Draining events every frame is not optional.
Colors are RGB tuples.
(255, 0, 0) is red, (0, 0, 0) black, (255, 255, 255) white. A fourth value is alpha: (255, 0, 0, 128) is half-transparent red (only on surfaces that support alpha).
🎮 Build This: Blank Window

A titled 800x600 window filled with a color that quits cleanly on the close button.

  1. Make a venv and pip install pygame-ce.
  2. Copy the four-step program above into main.py.
  3. Change the caption to your game's name and the fill to your favorite RGB color.
  4. Run python main.py and confirm the X button closes it without errors.

title: The Game Loop & Clock
html:
A game is a loop

The loop you wrote in 5.1 ran as fast as the CPU allowed — possibly thousands of pointless frames per second, pinning a core and draining the battery. A real game loop does three things every iteration at a controlled pace: process input, update state, render. The pacing comes from pygame.time.Clock.

Clock and a frame budget

Create one Clock before the loop. Call clock.tick(fps) once per frame. It sleeps just long enough to cap your loop at the target FPS, and it returns the number of milliseconds since the last call — your raw delta time.

loop.py
clock = pygame.time.Clock() FPS = 60 while running: dt = clock.tick(FPS) / 1000.0 # ms -> seconds for event in pygame.event.get(): if event.type == pygame.QUIT: running = False update(dt) # move things by speed * dt render(screen) pygame.display.flip()

Why delta time matters

If you move a dot with x += 5 every frame, it travels 5px/frame. On a 60Hz monitor that is 300px/sec; on a 144Hz monitor it is 720px/sec — your game runs at different speeds on different machines. The fix is to express speed in pixels per second and multiply by dt:

movement
x += 5 # framerate-DEPENDENT, wrong x += 200 * dt # 200 px/sec, framerate-independent
Use floats for position.
dt is fractional, so 200 * dt is rarely a whole number. Track position in float variables and only round to int when you draw. If you store position as int you will lose sub-pixel motion and slow movement will stutter or stop entirely.

The moving-dot demo

dot.py
import pygame, sys pygame.init() screen = pygame.display.set_mode((800, 600)) clock = pygame.time.Clock() x, y = 0.0, 300.0 speed = 250.0 # pixels per second running = True while running: dt = clock.tick(60) / 1000.0 for event in pygame.event.get(): if event.type == pygame.QUIT: running = False x += speed * dt if x > 800: x = 0.0 # wrap to the left edge screen.fill((15, 15, 25)) pygame.draw.circle(screen, (240, 200, 60), (int(x), int(y)), 20) pygame.display.flip() pygame.quit(); sys.exit()

Measuring real FPS

Call clock.get_fps() to see the smoothed actual frame rate. If it sits well below your target, your update/render is doing too much work per frame — profile before optimizing.

tick() caps, it does not guarantee.
If a frame takes longer than the budget (16.6ms at 60fps), dt grows. A huge dt after a stall (alt-tab, breakpoint) can teleport objects through walls. Clamp it: dt = min(dt, 0.05).
The loop order is sacred.
Events first (so input is fresh), then update (logic moves the world), then render (draw the result), then flip. Never draw before you have processed input for the frame.
🎮 Build This: Moving Dot

A circle that slides across the window at a steady, framerate-independent rate.

  1. Add a Clock and compute dt from tick(60).
  2. Store x as a float; update with x += speed * dt.
  3. Wrap to 0 when x > width.
  4. Draw with pygame.draw.circle using int(x); flip the display.
  5. Print clock.get_fps() once a second and confirm it sits near 60.

title: Drawing & Loading Images
html:
Two ways to put pixels on screen

You either draw primitives (rectangles, circles, lines) with the pygame.draw module, or you blit surfaces (loaded images, rendered text) onto the display. Both write to a Surface; the display surface is just the special one the window shows.

Drawing primitives

draw.py
pygame.draw.rect(screen, (255,255,255), (x, y, w, h)) # filled rect pygame.draw.rect(screen, (255,255,255), rect, width=2) # outline, 2px pygame.draw.circle(screen, (240,200,60), (cx, cy), radius) pygame.draw.line(screen, (90,90,120), (x1,y1), (x2,y2), 3)

The rect argument can be a 4-tuple (x, y, w, h) or a pygame.Rect. Pass width for an outline; omit it (or pass 0) for a filled shape.

Loading and blitting images

pygame.image.load(path) returns a Surface. Always call .convert() or .convert_alpha() on it immediately. Loaded images are in the file's pixel format; convert rematches them to the display's format, which makes every later blit dramatically faster.

images.py
player_img = pygame.image.load("assets/player.png").convert_alpha() bg_img = pygame.image.load("assets/bg.png").convert() # no transparency screen.blit(bg_img, (0, 0)) # background first screen.blit(player_img, (px, py)) # then the player on top
convert() needs a display.
You can only call .convert() after set_mode() has created the window. Calling it earlier raises pygame.error: cannot convert without pygame.display initialized. Use convert_alpha() for PNGs with transparency; plain convert() drops the alpha channel.
Use file paths that survive packaging.
Build paths relative to the script with os.path.join(os.path.dirname(__file__), "assets", "player.png"). Hard-coded relative paths break the moment you run from another folder or bundle with PyInstaller (covered in 5.8).

Text is a rendered image too

text.py
font = pygame.font.SysFont("consolas", 48) label = font.render("PONG", True, (255,255,255)) # True = antialiased rect = label.get_rect(center=(400, 50)) screen.blit(label, rect)

Pong rendered with shapes

Pong needs no art at all — two paddle rects and a ball. We will give it input and physics in the next lesson; here we just lay out and draw the board.

pong_draw.py
WIDTH, HEIGHT = 800, 600 WHITE = (235, 235, 235) left = pygame.Rect(30, HEIGHT//2 - 50, 12, 100) right = pygame.Rect(WIDTH-42, HEIGHT//2 - 50, 12, 100) ball = pygame.Rect(WIDTH//2 - 8, HEIGHT//2 - 8, 16, 16) while running: dt = clock.tick(60) / 1000.0 for e in pygame.event.get(): if e.type == pygame.QUIT: running = False screen.fill((10, 10, 15)) pygame.draw.aaline(screen, (60,60,70), (WIDTH//2,0), (WIDTH//2,HEIGHT)) # net pygame.draw.rect(screen, WHITE, left) pygame.draw.rect(screen, WHITE, right) pygame.draw.ellipse(screen, WHITE, ball) pygame.display.flip()
Painter's algorithm.
Pygame has no z-ordering. Whatever you blit last lands on top. Draw back-to-front every frame: background, then world objects, then UI text.
🎮 Build This: Pong Board (visuals)

Draw the full Pong scene: two paddles, a ball, a center net, and a score line.

  1. Define three pygame.Rects for left paddle, right paddle, and ball.
  2. Each frame, fill the background and draw the net with aaline.
  3. Draw the paddles as filled rects and the ball as an ellipse.
  4. Render a score string with a SysFont and blit it centered at the top.
  5. Confirm the layout looks right before adding movement next lesson.

title: Events & Input Handling
html:
Two input models, used together

Pygame gives you input in two flavors, and good games use both:

  • Events are discrete, one-time notifications in a queue: a key was pressed this frame, the window was closed, the mouse was clicked. Read them with pygame.event.get(). Use events for things that should happen once per press: jump, fire, pause, menu select.
  • Polled state is the snapshot of what is held down right now: pygame.key.get_pressed(), pygame.mouse.get_pos(). Use it for continuous actions: holding a paddle up, walking left.
Don't poll for one-shot actions.
If you fire a bullet whenever get_pressed()[K_SPACE] is true, you spew dozens of bullets per second from a single tap. Fire on the KEYDOWN event instead. Conversely, don't move a paddle on KEYDOWN events — you'll get one nudge, not smooth motion.

Reading the event queue

events.py
for event in pygame.event.get(): if event.type == pygame.QUIT: running = False elif event.type == pygame.KEYDOWN: if event.key == pygame.K_ESCAPE: running = False elif event.key == pygame.K_SPACE: serve_ball() # fires exactly once elif event.type == pygame.MOUSEBUTTONDOWN: print("click at", event.pos, "button", event.button)

Reading held keys

held.py
keys = pygame.key.get_pressed() # call once per frame if keys[pygame.K_w]: left.y -= PADDLE_SPEED * dt if keys[pygame.K_s]: left.y += PADDLE_SPEED * dt if keys[pygame.K_UP]: right.y -= PADDLE_SPEED * dt if keys[pygame.K_DOWN]: right.y += PADDLE_SPEED * dt
Rect coordinates are ints.
rect.y stores integers. Adding PADDLE_SPEED * dt (a float) truncates each frame, which is fine for fast paddles. For slow, precise objects keep a float position and assign rect.y = int(fy). We'll lean on that in Snake.

Finishing Pong

Now we combine both models: held keys drive the paddles continuously, SPACE serves the ball once, the ball bounces off the top/bottom walls and the paddles, and a miss scores a point and re-centers the ball.

pong.py
PADDLE_SPEED = 420.0 bvx, bvy = 0.0, 0.0 # ball velocity, starts unserved score_l = score_r = 0 def serve(): global bvx, bvy ball.center = (WIDTH//2, HEIGHT//2) bvx, bvy = 300.0, 200.0 while running: dt = clock.tick(60) / 1000.0 for e in pygame.event.get(): if e.type == pygame.QUIT: running = False elif e.type == pygame.KEYDOWN and e.key == pygame.K_SPACE: if bvx == 0: serve() # serve only when idle keys = pygame.key.get_pressed() if keys[pygame.K_w]: left.y -= int(PADDLE_SPEED*dt) if keys[pygame.K_s]: left.y += int(PADDLE_SPEED*dt) if keys[pygame.K_UP]: right.y -= int(PADDLE_SPEED*dt) if keys[pygame.K_DOWN]: right.y += int(PADDLE_SPEED*dt) left.clamp_ip(screen.get_rect()) # keep on-screen right.clamp_ip(screen.get_rect()) ball.x += int(bvx*dt); ball.y += int(bvy*dt) if ball.top <= 0 or ball.bottom >= HEIGHT: bvy = -bvy if ball.colliderect(left) or ball.colliderect(right): bvx = -bvx if ball.left <= 0: score_r += 1; bvx = 0 if ball.right >= WIDTH: score_l += 1; bvx = 0 screen.fill((10,10,15)) pygame.draw.rect(screen, (235,235,235), left) pygame.draw.rect(screen, (235,235,235), right) pygame.draw.ellipse(screen, (235,235,235), ball) pygame.display.flip()
clamp_ip keeps things in bounds.
rect.clamp_ip(area) moves a Rect (in place) so it stays inside another Rect — perfect for keeping paddles on screen without manual min/max math.
🎮 Build This: Playable Pong

Two-player Pong with held-key paddles and a space-to-serve ball.

  1. Drive both paddles with get_pressed() (W/S and Up/Down).
  2. Serve the ball only on the KEYDOWN SPACE event when it's idle.
  3. Bounce bvy off top/bottom; bounce bvx on paddle colliderect.
  4. Score and reset the ball when it passes a side edge.
  5. Render the live score with a font at the top center.

title: Rects & Movement
html:
The Rect is Pygame's workhorse

pygame.Rect(x, y, width, height) represents an axis-aligned rectangle. It is used for positions, collision, and layout. Its real power is the dozen named edge/anchor attributes you can read and assign:

AttributeMeaning
x, y / left, topTop-left corner
right, bottomFar edges
centerx, centery, centerCenter point
width, height, sizeDimensions
topleft, midbottom, ...9 anchor points

Assigning to any of these moves the rect without resizing it. rect.center = (400, 300) recenters; rect.bottom = HEIGHT snaps it to the floor. This makes positioning declarative and bug-resistant.

move vs move_ip.
rect.move(dx, dy) returns a new rect; rect.move_ip(dx, dy) mutates in place. Use move when you want to test a destination before committing (great for collision lookahead).

Grid-based movement

Snake doesn't move continuously — it hops cell by cell on a timer. Design the grid first: pick a CELL size and derive the playfield in cells. Every snake segment is one cell-sized Rect snapped to the grid.

grid.py
CELL = 24 COLS, ROWS = 30, 22 WIDTH, HEIGHT = COLS*CELL, ROWS*CELL # 720 x 528 # snake is a list of (col,row) cells, head first snake = [(15, 11), (14, 11), (13, 11)] direction = (1, 0) # moving right, in grid units

A movement timer, not per-frame motion

The loop still runs at 60 FPS for smooth input, but the snake only steps every STEP seconds. Accumulate dt and step when the accumulator overflows.

snake_move.py
STEP = 0.12 # seconds per move acc = 0.0 while running: dt = clock.tick(60) / 1000.0 acc += dt for e in pygame.event.get(): if e.type == pygame.QUIT: running = False elif e.type == pygame.KEYDOWN: if e.key == pygame.K_UP and direction != (0,1): next_dir = (0,-1) elif e.key == pygame.K_DOWN and direction != (0,-1): next_dir = (0,1) elif e.key == pygame.K_LEFT and direction != (1,0): next_dir = (-1,0) elif e.key == pygame.K_RIGHT and direction != (-1,0): next_dir = (1,0) if acc >= STEP: acc -= STEP direction = next_dir hx, hy = snake[0] new_head = (hx + direction[0], hy + direction[1]) # wrap around the walls (toroidal) new_head = (new_head[0] % COLS, new_head[1] % ROWS) snake.insert(0, new_head) snake.pop() # remove tail unless we ate (next lesson)
Buffer the direction; reject 180-degree turns.
Read input into a next_dir and only apply it at the step. If you mutate direction directly, a fast double-tap (up then left in one step) can reverse the snake into its own neck. Also block direct reversals: a snake moving right cannot instantly go left.

Rendering the grid snake

snake_draw.py
screen.fill((12, 18, 12)) for i, (c, r) in enumerate(snake): color = (120, 230, 120) if i == 0 else (70, 170, 70) cell = pygame.Rect(c*CELL, r*CELL, CELL-2, CELL-2) # -2 = gap pygame.draw.rect(screen, color, cell) pygame.display.flip()
Store grid coords, render pixel coords.
Keep game logic in cells (col,row) — integer math is exact and collision is just ==. Multiply by CELL only at draw time. Mixing pixels into logic invites off-by-one bugs.
🎮 Build This: Snake (movement)

A grid snake that steps on a timer, turns with the arrow keys, and wraps at the walls.

  1. Define CELL, COLS, ROWS; size the window from them.
  2. Hold the snake as a list of (col,row) cells; head at index 0.
  3. Buffer arrow input into next_dir, blocking 180-degree reversals.
  4. Accumulate dt; every STEP seconds insert a new head and pop the tail.
  5. Wrap the head with % COLS / % ROWS and draw each cell as a Rect.

title: Collision: Rect & Mask
html:
Rect collision: fast and good enough

Most 2D games run entirely on axis-aligned rectangle overlap tests. They are cheap and the API is clean:

  • a.colliderect(b) — do two Rects overlap? Returns bool.
  • rect.collidepoint(x, y) — is a point inside? (mouse hit-testing).
  • rect.collidelist(rects) — index of the first overlapping rect, or -1.
  • rect.collidelistall(rects) — indices of all overlaps.
rectcol.py
if player.colliderect(coin): score += 1 hit = bullet.collidelist(enemy_rects) if hit != -1: del enemy_rects[hit]

Mask collision: pixel-perfect

Rects are too generous for irregular shapes — a triangular ship's bounding box overlaps long before the visible pixels touch. A mask is a 1-bit-per-pixel map of an image's non-transparent pixels. mask.overlap(other, offset) returns the first colliding point (or None), accounting only for solid pixels.

maskcol.py
ship_mask = pygame.mask.from_surface(ship_surf) # needs alpha rock_mask = pygame.mask.from_surface(rock_surf) offset = (rock_rect.x - ship_rect.x, rock_rect.y - ship_rect.y) if ship_mask.overlap(rock_mask, offset): explode()
Broad phase, then narrow phase.
Masks are heavier than rects. The pro pattern is: first do a cheap colliderect; only if that passes do the precise mask.overlap. This keeps thousands of objects fast while staying pixel-accurate where it counts.
Masks come from alpha.
pygame.mask.from_surface reads transparency, so the source surface must be loaded with convert_alpha(). A fully opaque surface produces a solid rectangular mask — identical to a rect test, just slower. Rebuild the mask if you rotate/scale the image; the old mask no longer matches.

Finishing Snake

Snake needs exactly two collision rules, and on a grid both are simple equality checks on cells — no Rect needed for the logic, just for drawing. Eating food: the new head equals the food cell. Dying: the new head is already in the body.

snake_collide.py
import random def spawn_food(snake): while True: f = (random.randrange(COLS), random.randrange(ROWS)) if f not in snake: # never spawn on the snake return f food = spawn_food(snake) score = 0 # inside the STEP block, after computing new_head: if new_head in snake: # ran into own body -> game over running = False else: snake.insert(0, new_head) if new_head == food: # ate: grow (skip the pop) score += 1 food = spawn_food(snake) else: snake.pop() # normal: tail follows

The growth trick is elegant: every step you insert a head. Normally you also pop the tail, keeping length constant. When you eat, you skip the pop, so the snake grows by exactly one cell. No separate length counter needed.

Check self-collision before inserting.
Test new_head in snake while the old body is intact. If you insert the head first, the head trivially equals itself and you'd never detect a real crash. Order matters.

If you wanted rect-based collision instead

For a non-grid game you'd compare actual Rects: build a food_rect and head_rect at pixel positions and call head_rect.colliderect(food_rect). On a grid the cell-equality test is faster and exact, which is why we prefer it here.

🎮 Build This: Complete Snake

Add food and death to your timer-driven snake.

  1. Write spawn_food that picks a random empty cell (not on the snake).
  2. Each step, compute new_head first.
  3. If new_head in snake, end the game.
  4. Otherwise insert the head; if it equals food, grow (skip the pop) and respawn food; else pop the tail.
  5. Draw the food cell in a contrasting color and show the live score.

title: The Sprite Class, Groups, Sound & Music
html:
Stop tracking objects by hand

Managing parallel lists of positions, velocities, and rects gets unmanageable past a few entities. Pygame's sprite module fixes this. A Sprite is any object with two attributes: .image (a Surface) and .rect (its position). A Group is a container that can update() and draw() all its sprites at once.

sprite.py
class Invader(pygame.sprite.Sprite): def __init__(self, x, y, img): super().__init__() # REQUIRED self.image = img self.rect = self.image.get_rect(topleft=(x, y)) def update(self, dt, dx): self.rect.x += int(dx * dt) # group passes args through class Bullet(pygame.sprite.Sprite): def __init__(self, x, y): super().__init__() self.image = pygame.Surface((4, 12)); self.image.fill((255,255,0)) self.rect = self.image.get_rect(center=(x, y)) def update(self, dt, *_): self.rect.y -= int(500 * dt) if self.rect.bottom < 0: self.kill() # remove from ALL groups
super().__init__() is mandatory.
The base Sprite constructor sets up the internal group bookkeeping. Forget it and adding the sprite to a group raises AttributeError. And self.kill() is how a sprite removes itself — no manual list deletion.

Groups: update and draw everything

groups.py
invaders = pygame.sprite.Group() bullets = pygame.sprite.Group() for row in range(4): for col in range(10): invaders.add(Invader(60 + col*60, 60 + row*50, invader_img)) # in the loop: invaders.update(dt, dx) # calls every invader's update(dt,dx) bullets.update(dt) invaders.draw(screen) # blits every .image at its .rect bullets.draw(screen)

Group-to-group collision

This is where Groups shine. groupcollide tests every sprite in one group against another and returns a dict of the collisions, optionally killing the hit sprites.

collide.py
# kill bullet AND invader on hit; dict maps bullet -> [invaders hit] hits = pygame.sprite.groupcollide(bullets, invaders, True, True) for bullet in hits: score += 10 explosion_snd.play() # single sprite vs group (player vs enemy bullets) if pygame.sprite.spritecollideany(player, enemy_bullets): lives -= 1

Sound effects and music

The mixer handles two separate things. Sounds (short SFX) are loaded fully into memory as pygame.mixer.Sound objects and played on channels — many at once. Music (long background tracks) is streamed from disk via pygame.mixer.music — one track at a time.

audio.py
pygame.mixer.pre_init(44100, -16, 2, 512) # before pygame.init(): low latency pygame.init() shoot_snd = pygame.mixer.Sound("sfx/shoot.wav") explosion_snd = pygame.mixer.Sound("sfx/boom.wav") shoot_snd.set_volume(0.4) shoot_snd.play() # fire-and-forget, overlapping pygame.mixer.music.load("music/loop.ogg") pygame.mixer.music.set_volume(0.3) pygame.mixer.music.play(-1) # -1 = loop forever
Audio latency and formats.
If sounds lag behind the action, lower the mixer buffer with pre_init(44100, -16, 2, 512) before pygame.init(). Prefer .ogg and .wav; MP3 support is inconsistent across platforms. Don't reload a Sound every shot — load it once at startup.
Sound = short & many; music = long & one.
Use mixer.Sound for effects you fire repeatedly and overlap, and mixer.music for a single streamed background track. Mixing these up wastes memory or cuts off your SFX.
🎮 Build This: Space Invaders

Sprite-grouped invaders and bullets with group collisions and audio.

  1. Make Player, Invader, and Bullet subclasses of Sprite with .image and .rect.
  2. Fill an invaders Group in a grid; march them sideways and drop down at the edges.
  3. Spawn a Bullet into a group on the SPACE KEYDOWN event; play shoot_snd.
  4. Each frame run groupcollide(bullets, invaders, True, True), add score, play explosion_snd.
  5. End the game if spritecollideany(player, enemy_bullets) or invaders reach the bottom; loop background music with play(-1).

title: Capstone: A Platformer, Scenes & Packaging
html:
Scenes: one game, many states

A real game is not one loop — it is a menu, then gameplay, then a game-over screen, each with its own input, update, and draw. The clean pattern is a Scene base class and a tiny manager that runs the current scene and lets scenes request a switch.

scene.py
class Scene: def __init__(self): self.next = self # stay on self by default def handle(self, events): pass def update(self, dt): pass def draw(self, screen): pass def switch(self, scene): self.next = scene class MenuScene(Scene): def handle(self, events): for e in events: if e.type == pygame.KEYDOWN and e.key == pygame.K_RETURN: self.switch(PlayScene()) def draw(self, screen): screen.fill((20,20,35)) # blit "PRESS ENTER" text here class GameOverScene(Scene): def __init__(self, score): super().__init__(); self.score = score def handle(self, events): for e in events: if e.type == pygame.KEYDOWN and e.key == pygame.K_RETURN: self.switch(MenuScene())
main.py (manager)
scene = MenuScene() while running: dt = clock.tick(60) / 1000.0 events = pygame.event.get() for e in events: if e.type == pygame.QUIT: running = False scene.handle(events) scene.update(dt) scene.draw(screen) pygame.display.flip() scene = scene.next # switch takes effect next frame

Platformer physics in PlayScene

A platformer adds gravity, jumping, and one-way-blocking collision against tiles. The classic trick is axis-separated collision: move on X and resolve X overlaps, then move on Y and resolve Y overlaps. Resolving both at once produces sticky corners and tunneling.

platformer.py
GRAVITY = 2000.0 # px/s^2 JUMP = -650.0 # upward velocity vx, vy = 0.0, 0.0 on_ground = False keys = pygame.key.get_pressed() vx = (keys[pygame.K_RIGHT] - keys[pygame.K_LEFT]) * 300.0 if keys[pygame.K_SPACE] and on_ground: vy = JUMP; on_ground = False vy += GRAVITY * dt # --- X axis --- player.x += round(vx * dt) for t in tiles: if player.colliderect(t): if vx > 0: player.right = t.left elif vx < 0: player.left = t.right # --- Y axis --- player.y += round(vy * dt) on_ground = False for t in tiles: if player.colliderect(t): if vy > 0: player.bottom = t.top; on_ground = True; vy = 0 elif vy < 0: player.top = t.bottom; vy = 0
Snap to the edge you hit.
When colliding, don't just stop — set the matching anchor (player.bottom = t.top) so the player rests exactly on the surface. Then zero the velocity on that axis. Setting on_ground only inside the downward-Y branch gives you reliable jump gating.

Packaging with PyInstaller

To ship a single double-clickable file, freeze the game with PyInstaller. Install it, then bundle. The critical flag is --add-data, which copies your assets into the bundle.

terminal
$ pip install pyinstaller $ pyinstaller --onefile --windowed \ --add-data "assets:assets" \ --add-data "sfx:sfx" main.py # use ; instead of : on Windows $ ./dist/main # the standalone executable
Assets vanish after bundling.
A frozen app unpacks to a temp folder, so plain relative paths break. Resolve assets through PyInstaller's sys._MEIPASS: base = getattr(sys, "_MEIPASS", os.path.dirname(__file__)), then os.path.join(base, "assets", "player.png"). The --add-data separator is : on macOS/Linux but ; on Windows.
--windowed hides the console.
Use --windowed (alias --noconsole) so a black terminal doesn't pop up behind your game. Build with the console first to read tracebacks, then rebuild windowed for release. Test the executable on a clean machine without Python installed.
🎮 Build This: Platformer Capstone + Executable

A small platformer with menu, play, and game-over scenes, packaged into a standalone app.

  1. Build the Scene base class and a manager loop that does scene = scene.next.
  2. Implement Menu (ENTER to play), Play (gravity + jump + axis-separated tile collision), and GameOver (ENTER to menu) scenes.
  3. Add a goal/coins and pass the final score into GameOverScene.
  4. Load all assets through a _MEIPASS-aware path helper.
  5. Package with pyinstaller --onefile --windowed --add-data and run dist/ to confirm assets and audio load.
